tldr: Aider is a terminal-based AI pair programmer that edits local files via CLI, while Bolt.new is a browser-based AI full-stack environment that scaffolds and runs apps from prompts. Migration means moving from a CLI-driven workflow against your own repo to a hosted in-browser IDE.
why_switch: Skip local environment setup entirely — the runtime lives in the browser
Run, preview, and deploy from a single hosted workspace
Faster scaffolding for greenfield apps and prototypes
Built-in full-stack runtime with package install and preview handled for you
Lower barrier to entry for teammates who don't live in a terminal
what_to_export: Local repository state and Git history
Aider configuration (model selection, edit mode, settings)
Conversation transcripts if you retain them
Prompt or instruction templates used with Aider
Any custom scripts that wrap Aider
Environment variables and API key sources
export_method: Aider operates against a local Git repository, so the bulk of your export is already done: code and history live in your repo. Capture your Aider configuration file (typically a project-level config plus environment variables for your model provider), any wrapper scripts, and your prompt templates. If you keep transcripts of Aider sessions, archive them locally. Note which model backends you use and where their API keys come from. Document conventions like commit-on-edit behavior and any pre-commit hooks Aider relied on, since those will need a different home in a hosted environment.
import_method: In Bolt.new, start by importing or pasting your existing project, or scaffold a fresh app and copy code in. The browser IDE handles dependencies, runtime, and preview, so you typically configure project structure and let it install. Wire up environment variables in the project settings for any external API keys you previously fed Aider. Recreate prompt templates as in-IDE instructions or saved snippets. For team workflows, connect Bolt.new to your Git remote so changes flow back to your canonical repo. Validate that your test suite and scripts still run inside the hosted runtime, and adjust any commands that assumed a local terminal. Verify model availability, plan tiers, and runtime limits in current Bolt.new docs before standardizing the workflow.

concept_mapping.note: Sync via Git remote to keep canonical history
Available models depend on the platform — confirm in current docs

gotchas: Bolt.new runs in a hosted browser environment, so workflows that assume a local filesystem or system tools may need adaptation
Aider works against any LLM backend you configure; Bolt.new's available models and limits depend on the platform — verify in current docs
Long-running processes or specialized native dependencies may not behave the same in a browser runtime
Aider commits directly to Git; verify Bolt.new's commit and sync model matches your team's expectations
Cost models differ: Aider is OSS with user-paid model usage, while Bolt.new bundles tiers — consult target's official pricing page for current plans and any usage limits
Confirm data export and code portability options in current Bolt.new docs before committing the team's primary workflow

email.subject: Dev workflow change: moving from Aider to Bolt.new over 14 days
email.body:
Hi there,

A quick heads up that over the next two weeks our team will be moving its AI-assisted development workflow from Aider to Bolt.new. The motivation is to reduce local setup overhead and let more of the team collaborate on prototypes directly in the browser.

What this means for you:
- Nothing changes in the product you use day-to-day; this is an internal tooling change.
- Code review and release cadence stay the same.
- You may see slightly faster iteration on small features as we settle in.

We will validate Bolt.new's current plan tiers and runtime limits against our needs on the official pricing and docs pages before standardizing. If you notice anything unusual in releases during this period, just reply and we'll investigate.

Thanks for being with us.

Your customer success team
